Enforcement 
(916) 322.5662 
322·5660 
322.5901 
322-6441 
Steven S. Wall 
Professional Corporation 
July 10, 1985 
Luce, Forward, Hamilton & Scripps 
The Bank of California Plaza 
110 West A Street 
San Diego, CA 
92101 
Dear Mr. Wall: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-8S-lSl 
You have asked whether Mr. T. M. Cromwell, Senior Vice President 
of Borax, has any potential conflict of interest if he testifies in 
a Public utilities Commission hearing on behalf of Borax. 
Mr. Cromwell is a member of the California Mining and Geology Board. 
The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from 
making, participating in, or using his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he has a financial 
interest. 
Government Code Section 87100. 
Simply by testifying in a 
P.U.C. proceeding, Mr. Cromwell would not be. making or participating 
in the P.U.C. decision. 
Furthermore, he would not be "using his 
official position to influence" the P.U.C. decision merely by 
testifying on behalf of Borax. 
That term only applies when a public 
official attempts to influence a decision of his own agency or 
another agency over which his agency has appointive or budgetary 
control, or when the official represents (or purports to represent) 
the position of his own agency before an unrelated agency. 
2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18700.1. 
Since the Mining and Geology Board has no appointive or 
budgetary control over the P.U.C., and assuming that Mr. Cromwell 
would be testifying solely as a representative of Borax, and not as 
a representative of the Mining Board, his testimony before the 
P.U.C. would not involve any conflict of interest under the 
Political Reform Act.

!~~neral Counsel 
I

TELECOPIER: (619) 232·8311 
July 3, 1985 
Barbara Milman, Esq. 
General Counsel 
California Fair Political Practices Commission 
100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 95814 
LA.JOLLA 
7817 IVANHOe: AVENUE, SUITE 201 
LA JOLLA, CALIFORN1A 92037 
(els) 459-40H 
NORTH COUNTY 
GRAHAM INTERNAT!ONAL PLAZA 
211! PALOMAR AIRPORT ROAD, SUITE: 300 
CARLSBAD, CALIFORNIA 92008-4815 
(SIS) 438·7333 
Re: 
Potential Conflict of Interest; United States 
Borax & Chemical Corporation; Testimony of 
T 
M Cromwell 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
Earlier this week I spoke with Dan Stanford about the 
potential conflict of interest question raised by our client, 
United States Borax & Chemical Corporation, in connection with 
testimony that will be presented on its behalf to the Public 
Utilities Commission through Mr. T. M Cromwell, the Senior 
Vice President of Borax. 
Dan suggested that I write you this 
letter confirming the nature of our questions and requesting 
a written opinion from you. 
Mr. Cromwell will be testifying on behalf of U.S. Borax 
in certain hearings being conducted before the PUC's Adminis-
trative Law Judge in Case No. all #84-04-079 
Recently, how-
ever, Mr. Cromwell has been appointed by the Governor to serve 
as a member of the California Mining and Geology Board 
In 
filling in his application for that position, Mr. Cromwell indi-
cated that he occasionally testified before governmental agen-
cies, such as the PUC, in connection with matters of importance 
to U.S 
Borax 
The question we raise is whether or not there is any con-
flict of interest in Mr 
Cromwell appearing and presenting tes-
timony before the PUC. 
Dan Stanford has indicated that your 
analysis indicates that no such conflict exists 
I would 


LUCE, FORWARD, HAMILTON & SCRIPPS 
Barbara Milman, Esq. 
July 3, 1985 
page 2 
appreciate your confirming this to me in a letter. 
Thank you very much for your courtesy and cooperation.

Dan Stanford 
Yours very truly, 
Steven S. wall

sections.question: You have asked whether Mr. T. M. Cromwell, Senior Vice President of Borax, has any potential conflict of interest if he testifies in a Public utilities Commission hearing on behalf of Borax.
sections.conclusion: Since the Mining and Geology Board has no appointive or budgetary control over the P.U.C., and assuming that Mr. Cromwell would be testifying solely as a representative of Borax, and not as a representative of the Mining Board, his testimony before the P.U.C. would not involve any conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.

sections.question_synthetic: Does Mr. T.M. Cromwell, a Senior Vice President of Borax and member of the California Mining and Geology Board, have a conflict of interest if he testifies before the Public Utilities Commission on behalf of Borax?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Mr. Cromwell's testimony before the P.U.C. on behalf of Borax does not constitute a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act because he would not be making or participating in the P.U.C. decision, nor using his official position to influence it. The Mining and Geology Board has no appointive or budgetary control over the P.U.C., and his testimony is as a Borax representative, not as a Mining Board representative.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (July 3, 1985) from counsel and the FPPC response letter (July 10, 1985). Clear Q&C sections present. OCR format noted in parsing notes but text is legible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether Mr. T. M. Cromwell, Senior Vice President of Borax, has any potential conflict of interest if he testifies in a Public utilities Commission hearing on behalf of Borax.

CONCLUSION: Since the Mining and Geology Board has no appointive or budgetary control over the P.U.C., and assuming that Mr. Cromwell would be testifying solely as a representative of Borax, and not as a representative of the Mining Board, his testimony before the P.U.C. would not involve any conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether T.M. Cromwell, a Senior Vice President of Borax and member of the California Mining and Geology Board, has a conflict of interest if he testifies before the Public Utilities Commission on behalf of Borax. The FPPC concluded no conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act.
